narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40 and an LSAT score of 39, you've built an impressive academic foundation, including strong Year 1 and Year 3 decile rankings of 7 in your school, along with your upper-middle family income background and plans to attend full-time. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 49% probability of failure for you, which crossed the threshold leading to the rejection of your law school application. This prediction stems from a careful analysis of how your profile stacks up against thousands of past students' data. The most influential factor pulling in a positive direction—toward predicting you'd pass—was your Year 3 decile ranking, which stands above the typical student's average and signaled strong late-stage academic momentum. However, your upper-middle family income bracket had the strongest push toward predicting failure, as it deviated in a way that the model associates with higher risk compared to the dataset's averages. Your undergraduate GPA followed closely as another key factor nudging the prediction toward failure, even though your 3.40 is above average, suggesting the model weighs it heavily in context with others. Meanwhile, your LSAT score, full-time status, and Year 1 decile provided smaller counterbalances toward passing, but they weren't enough to offset the top concerns. Overall, your profile is stronger than many in areas like LSAT and GPA, but the model flagged family income and GPA as standout risks relative to students who typically pass the bar. I know this rejection stings, especially with your promising metrics, and it's okay to feel disappointed—many successful applicants face setbacks like this. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to push it even higher or gathering more evidence of your Year 3 performance trajectory, perhaps through additional recommendations. You might also explore programs that weigh family background less heavily or offer bar prep support. With your full-time commitment and academic deciles, refining these areas could tip the scales next time—keep pursuing your goals, as your potential shines through. We're rooting for you.
